title: Add restricted project-understanding assistant with versioned evidence records
notes:
Created from human suggestion #24 (submitted by mannyrayner on 2026-05-30) and broadened after follow-up discussion. The requested capability is an admin/trusted-user project-understanding assistant that answers high-level questions about publicly available aspects of C-LARA-2 using repository materials as evidence. Initial implementation work began on 2026-05-31 with a core prompt-wrapper/request function and Markdown evidence-record rendering helpers. By 2026-06-01 the architecture had been revised to delegate repository exploration to `codex exec` rather than a normal API/retrieval wrapper, and a first admin-only platform implementation was working: `src/core/project_understanding.py` builds the versioned prompt, invokes `codex exec` in read-only/non-interactive mode, passes `OPENAI_API_KEY` through a reduced environment, resolves common Windows npm Codex shims, captures elapsed time/stdout/stderr/exit status/model/prompt version/token count where extractable from stdout or stderr, computes and labels a conservative output-priced upper-bound cost estimate when only total Codex tokens are available (with local OpenAI Usage comparisons currently suggesting the estimate can be at least three times higher than actual API charges), parses the final answer, and can render/write versionable records. The Django platform now exposes `/admin-tools/project-understanding/` for staff users, queues runs through Django Q, records `TaskUpdate` progress and heartbeat messages, persists request/result JSON under `MEDIA_ROOT/admin_project_understanding/`, and polls a status endpoint until the answer is available. Manual smoke tests produced plausible cited answers for repository-summary and annotated-text-format questions, demonstrating that Codex can inspect repository files itself. Remaining work includes an explicit export/review flow into `docs/project_understanding/`, reviewer assessment controls, citation/path sanitization for non-admin contexts, exact-cost reconciliation if Codex exposes input/cached-input/output usage data, hard budget/rate limiting, curated evaluation records for the report, and maintaining the now-resolved projects migration graph: the pre-existing `0036_profile_byok_fields` versus `0038_projectimagestyle_disallow_text_in_images` branch was merged in `0039`, and `0040` captures the remaining detected model-state changes so fresh-database admin-tool test runs are clean again. Original priority was P2: strategically important for project maintenance and the C-LARA-2 autonomy/authorship evidence case, but restricted until review, cost, prompt-injection, and evidence-quality controls are complete.

Update on 2026-06-14: production hardening work replaced the first in-Gunicorn thread execution path with a dedicated file-backed worker queue. Assistant requests now persist queued request records, the `process_project_understanding_queue` management command claims and runs them from a separate `project-understanding-worker` systemd service, and the monitor reports queue/worker state. AWS deployment testing confirmed successful in-platform project-understanding answers after configuring the worker unit with `Environment=HOME=/home/ubuntu`, `CODEX_HOME=/var/lib/c-lara/codex`, the existing Codex executable path, and the `bwrap-userns-restrict` AppArmor profile. The feature is therefore operational again on the server in a more robust service-owned context. Remaining ISSUE-0034 work is now focused on export/review records, reviewer assessment controls, budget/rate limiting, stale-lock/stale-running recovery, and curated evaluation evidence rather than basic server execution.

Update on 2026-06-23: human suggestion #25 reported a targeted production regression in the Assistant tab: repository-understanding queries that ask about the assistant/self-understanding feature itself, such as whether it is available or what the Assistant tab does, consistently fail with a Codex worker error saying the Linux sandbox/command execution layer failed and reporting `failed rtm_newaddr`. Other project-understanding queries have generally produced plausible answers, so this should be treated as a focused environment/instrumentation failure rather than a complete feature rollback. Follow-up should capture fuller worker diagnostics around bubblewrap/user-namespace/systemd/AppArmor/network-address setup, add a smoke/evaluation query that asks about the Assistant tab itself, and confirm whether the production worker profile differs from successful general repository-inspection runs. Priority is raised to P1 while this user-visible assistant self-query failure is unresolved, because it undermines confidence in the restricted assistant just as the feature is being evaluated. Follow-up implementation in the same 2026-06-23 triage pass updated `make run-platform-with-real-q` so laptop runs start the dedicated `process_project_understanding_queue` worker alongside the real Django-Q worker and dev server; this should make it possible to check whether the Assistant-tab/self-query failure reproduces outside AWS before deeper production-only diagnosis. A first laptop trial of the minimal query "Summarise the project briefly" showed the local worker path is now being exercised, but the Windows/Cygwin environment reported `bwrap=(not found)` and Codex failed before repository inspection; local reproduction therefore requires documenting and using a Codex-supported sandbox environment such as WSL2/Linux with `bubblewrap` installed, or another host where the Codex read-only sandbox works. Follow-up on the same triage thread now treats server-side diagnostics as the lower-effort first step: run paired `check_project_understanding_codex --smoke --question ...` checks on the server for a normal project-summary question and for the suspect Assistant self-query, and verify that the sandbox-failure detector is not misclassifying successful answers that merely quote issue-registry text containing `failed rtm_newaddr`. Server deployment of the first detector change showed the suspected false-positive pattern clearly: self-understanding answers can quote grep-style issue evidence such as `docs/issues/issues/ISSUE-0034.json:10: ... could not inspect ... failed rtm_newaddr`, and the detector still treated that repository quote as a live sandbox failure. The follow-up fix now ignores grep-style repository evidence lines while preserving direct runtime failures such as `bwrap: loopback: Failed RTM_NEWADDR: Operation not permitted`. A subsequent server run showed another false-positive class: self-understanding answers can quote the detector source code itself, including `low_level_bwrap_failure = "failed rtm_newaddr" in line_lower and (`. The detector now requires low-level RTM_NEWADDR matches to look like actual bwrap/bubblewrap diagnostics (`bwrap:`, `bubblewrap:`, or `loopback`) rather than arbitrary code identifiers, and error messages now say whether the detection came from Codex stdout or stderr. The next refinement adds a model-based review step after the heuristic fires: when an OpenAI key is available, a short classifier prompt inspects the Codex stdout/stderr and decides whether the transcript is a genuine live sandbox/access failure or a plausible answer that only quotes error-looking repository text. The heuristic remains a first-pass guard, but the reviewer can now allow plausible answers through and error messages include the reviewer verdict/reason.
